cited_case: Reorganized Debtors v. Hertz Corp. (In re Hertz Corp.), 2024 U.S. App. LEXIS 34433 (2024)

brief_sentence: claims for unmatured interest are disallowed in bankruptcy. Courts have often treated make-whole premiums as the economic equivalent of unmatured interest, leading to their disallowance in many cases . Reorganized Debtors v. Hertz Corp. (In re Hertz Corp.), 2024 U.S. App. LEXIS 34433 (2024), Wells Fargo Bank, N.A. v. Hertz Corp. (In re Hertz Corp.), 637 B.R. 781 (2021).
brief_block: claims for unmatured interest are disallowed in bankruptcy. Courts have often treated make-whole premiums as the economic equivalent of unmatured interest, leading to their disallowance in many cases. Reorganized Debtors v. Hertz Corp. (In re Hertz Corp.), 2024 U.S. App. LEXIS 34433 (2024), Wells Fargo Bank, N.A. v. Hertz Corp. (In re Hertz Corp.), 637 B.R. 781 (2021).
opinion_block: We determine that the Applicable Premiums must be disallowed under Section 502(b)(2), for they fit both the dictionary definition of interest and are its economic equivalent. ... To sum up, Section 502(b)(2) disallows a claim for unmatured interest if it is either definitionally interest or its economic equivalent. Because the Applicable Premiums are both, the Bankruptcy Court correctly disallowed the Noteholders' claims for those Premiums.
finding_analysis: The Third Circuit opinion directly supports the proposition. Judge Ambro held that make-whole fees (the Applicable Premiums) must be disallowed under Section 502(b)(2) because they are both definitionally interest and the economic equivalent of interest. The court adopted the 'economic equivalent' approach endorsed by the Fifth Circuit in Ultra Petroleum and concluded the Applicable Premiums were 'mathematically equivalent to the unmatured interest the Noteholders would have received had Hertz redeemed the Notes on their Redemption Dates.' The brief's framing that courts have 'treated make-whole premiums as the economic equivalent of unmatured interest, leading to their disallowance' is an accurate summary of the court's claim-allowance holding. (The Third Circuit separately held the premiums were payable under the solvent-debtor exception/absolute priority rule, but that does not undercut the claim-allowance holding the brief cites.)

brief_block: claims for unmatured interest are disallowed in bankruptcy. Courts have often treated make-whole premiums as the economic equivalent of unmatured interest, leading to their disallowance in many cases. Reorganized Debtors v. Hertz Corp. (In re Hertz Corp.), 2024 U.S. App. LEXIS 34433 (2024), Wells Fargo Bank, N.A. v. Hertz Corp. (In re Hertz Corp.), 637 B.R. 781 (2021).
opinion_block: Calling a make-whole a contract right or a liquidated damages provision does not answer the question of whether it is unmatured interest. ... Instead, the Court concludes that the determination of whether the redemption premium that Wells Fargo seeks in this case is, in fact, the economic equivalent of unmatured interest is not a legal question, but is instead a factual one ... the Court concludes that Wells Fargo may be able to present evidence that the redemption premium in the 2026/2028 Senior Notes is not, in fact, the economic equivalent of unmatured interest due under those Senior Notes.
finding_analysis: Judge Walrath's 2021 opinion supports the general proposition only partially. She acknowledged and surveyed case law treating make-whole premiums as economic equivalents of unmatured interest subject to disallowance under Section 502(b)(2), and held that courts 'look to the economic substance of the transaction to determine what counts as interest.' However, at the motion-to-dismiss stage she did NOT disallow the make-whole: she denied Hertz's motion as to the 2026/2028 Notes, holding that whether the premium is the economic equivalent of unmatured interest is a fact question requiring discovery. So the opinion supports the general doctrinal point that make-wholes can be and sometimes are disallowed as unmatured interest, but the 637 B.R. 781 ruling itself is not an example of disallowance at this stage (disallowance came later on summary judgment, which the 3d Cir affirmed in 2024). Citing this opinion for the proposition is defensible but slightly overstates what this particular ruling did.

proposition: The Second Circuit held that automatic acceleration of debt upon a bankruptcy filing changes the maturity date of the debt to the petition date, rendering any subsequent payment a post-maturity payment rather than a prepayment, so make-whole premiums tied to prepayment are not enforceable unless the indenture contains clear and unambiguous language requiring payment after acceleration.
cited_case: Apollo Global Mgmt., LLC v. Bokf, NA (In re MPM Silicones, L.L.C.), 874 F.3d 787 (2017)
retrieved_case: Momentive Performance Materials Inc. v. BOKF, NA
brief_sentence: For example, in , Apollo Global Mgmt., LLC v. Bokf, NA (In re MPM Silicones, L.L.C.), 874 F.3d 787 (2017). the Second Circuit held that automatic acceleration of debt upon a bankruptcy filing changes the maturity date of the debt to the petition date, rendering any subsequent payment a post-maturity payment rather than a prepayment. Consequently, make-whole premiums tied to prepayment were not enforceable unless the indenture contained clear and unambiguous language requiring payment of the premium even after acceleration . Apollo Global Mgmt., LLC v. Bokf, NA (In re MPM Silicones, L.L.C.), 874 F.3d 787 (2017).
brief_block: For example, in Apollo Global Mgmt., LLC v. Bokf, NA (In re MPM Silicones, L.L.C.), 874 F.3d 787 (2017), the Second Circuit held that automatic acceleration of debt upon a bankruptcy filing changes the maturity date of the debt to the petition date, rendering any subsequent payment a post-maturity payment rather than a prepayment. Consequently, make-whole premiums tied to prepayment were not enforceable unless the indenture contained clear and unambiguous language requiring payment of the premium even after acceleration.
opinion_block: American's bankruptcy petition triggered a default, and this default automatically accelerated the debt. That acceleration changed the date of maturity from some point in the future ... to an earlier date based on the debtor's default under the contract. ... We also held in AMR that acceleration brought about by a bankruptcy filing changes the date of maturity of the accelerated notes to the date of the petition. 730 F.3d at 103. Therefore, any payment on the accelerated notes following a bankruptcy filing would be a post-maturity payment. ... Here, Debtors' payment was post-maturity, not 'at or before' maturity.
finding_analysis: The Second Circuit opinion directly supports every element of the brief's proposition. Applying AMR, the court held that MPM's bankruptcy filing automatically accelerated the Senior-Lien Notes under Section 6.02, changing the maturity date to the petition date; thus any subsequent payment was post-maturity rather than a prepayment/optional redemption. Because the Optional Redemption Clauses only triggered the make-whole premium for redemptions 'at [MPM's] option' prior to maturity, and the payment was neither optional nor pre-maturity, no make-whole was owed. The court also rejected the noteholders' argument that general 'premium, if any' language in the Acceleration Clauses sufficed, confirming the brief's point that only clear and unambiguous indenture language providing for payment after acceleration could carry the make-whole in that posture.

proposition: In the Momentive case, the SDNY Bankruptcy Court denied enforcement of a make-whole premium, emphasizing that the indenture must explicitly provide for such payment following acceleration.
cited_case: Apollo Global Mgmt., LLC v. Bokf, NA (In re MPM Silicones, L.L.C.), 874 F.3d 787 (2017)
retrieved_case: Momentive Performance Materials Inc. v. BOKF, NA
brief_sentence: New York law generally enforces make-whole premiums as liquidated damages if they are reasonable and not a penalty. However, in bankruptcy, SDNY courts have consistently required clear and unambiguous language in the governing agreements to enforce make-whole premiums after acceleration. For instance, in Momentive, the SDNY Bankruptcy Court denied enforcement of a make-whole premium, emphasizing that the indenture must explicitly provide for such payment following acceleration . Apollo Global Mgmt., LLC v. Bokf, NA (In re MPM Silicones, L.L.C.), 874 F.3d 787 (2017), Del. Trust Co. v. Energy Future Intermediate Holding Co. LLC (In re Energy Future Holdings Corp.), 842 F.3d 247 (2016), 6.04 Delaware Court in Energy Future Holdings Case Follows SDNY in Denying Make-Whole Premium after Acceleration Based on Plain Language of Loan Documents.
brief_block: New York law generally enforces make-whole premiums as liquidated damages if they are reasonable and not a penalty. However, in bankruptcy, SDNY courts have consistently required clear and unambiguous language in the governing agreements to enforce make-whole premiums after acceleration. For instance, in Momentive, the SDNY Bankruptcy Court denied enforcement of a make-whole premium, emphasizing that the indenture must explicitly provide for such payment following acceleration. Apollo Global Mgmt., LLC v. Bokf, NA (In re MPM Silicones, L.L.C.), 874 F.3d 787 (2017), Del. Trust Co. v. Energy Future Intermediate Holding Co. LLC (In re Energy Future Holdings Corp.), 842 F.3d 247 (2016) [...]
opinion_block: The bankruptcy court concluded that the Senior-Lien Notes holders were not entitled to the premium. It reasoned that under the 2012 Indentures the make-whole premium would be due only in the case of an 'optional redemption' and not in the case of an acceleration brought about by a bankruptcy filing. 2014 WL 4436335, at *11-*15. The district court agreed. 531 B.R. at 335-38. We too agree.
finding_analysis: The brief's substantive description of the Momentive holding is accurate: the SDNY Bankruptcy Court (Judge Drain) denied enforcement of the make-whole because the 2012 Indentures' Optional Redemption Clauses did not trigger upon bankruptcy-caused automatic acceleration, and the court required indenture language that specifically provided for the make-whole after acceleration. The Second Circuit opinion cited (874 F.3d 787) recounts and affirms exactly that reasoning. The minor gap is that 874 F.3d 787 is the circuit affirmance, not the bankruptcy court opinion the brief invokes; a more precise cite would be to 2014 WL 4436335 (Bankr. S.D.N.Y.) or 531 B.R. 321 (the district court affirmance). Because the Second Circuit opinion expressly describes and endorses the bankruptcy court's reasoning, citing it is a defensible shortcut rather than a misattribution.

brief_block: New York law generally enforces make-whole premiums as liquidated damages if they are reasonable and not a penalty. However, in bankruptcy, SDNY courts have consistently required clear and unambiguous language in the governing agreements to enforce make-whole premiums after acceleration. For instance, in Momentive, the SDNY Bankruptcy Court denied enforcement of a make-whole premium, emphasizing that the indenture must explicitly provide for such payment following acceleration. [...] Del. Trust Co. v. Energy Future Intermediate Holding Co. LLC (In re Energy Future Holdings Corp.), 842 F.3d 247 (2016) [...]
opinion_block: We believe, however, the result in Momentive conflicts with that indenture's text and fails to honor the parties' bargain. For these and additional reasons discussed below, we find it unpersuasive. [...] Recently, however, bankruptcy courts, including the Bankruptcy Court here, have stretched Northwestern beyond its language and applied its clear-statement rule to yield-protection payments not styled as prepayment premiums.
finding_analysis: EFH is cited for the proposition that Momentive's clear-and-unambiguous-language rule is the governing approach, but the Third Circuit in EFH does the opposite: Judge Ambro expressly rejects Momentive as 'unpersuasive' and criticizes bankruptcy courts for 'stretch[ing] Northwestern beyond its language' by extending the clear-statement rule to make-wholes that are not styled as prepayment premiums. EFH holds that under New York law (per NML Capital), contract terms 'not necessarily impacted by acceleration . . . [do not] automatically cease to be enforceable after acceleration,' so the noteholders were entitled to the make-whole despite the absence of explicit post-acceleration language. Citing EFH for the Momentive rule therefore inverts the Third Circuit's actual holding. (Also note the proposition attributes the rule to 'SDNY courts,' but EFH is a Third Circuit opinion reviewing a Delaware bankruptcy case.)

proposition: Where an indenture explicitly states that the make-whole premium is payable even after acceleration, courts may enforce it, as in In re Madison 92nd St. Associates LLC where a prepayment premium was upheld under New York law because the agreement contained clear language requiring payment even after default and acceleration.
cited_case: Paloian v. LaSalle Bank N.A (In re Doctors Hosp. of Hyde Park, Inc.), 508 B.R. 697 (2014)
retrieved_case: Paloian v. LaSalle Bank National Ass'n (In re Doctors Hospital of Hyde Park, Inc.)
brief_sentence: Clear and Unambiguous Language: If the indenture explicitly states that the make-whole premium is payable even after acceleration, courts may enforce it. For example, in In re Madison 92nd St. Associates LLC, a prepayment premium was upheld under New York law because the agreement contained clear language requiring payment even after default and acceleration . Paloian v. LaSalle Bank N.A (In re Doctors Hosp. of Hyde Park, Inc.), 508 B.R. 697 (2014).
brief_block: Clear and Unambiguous Language: If the indenture explicitly states that the make-whole premium is payable even after acceleration, courts may enforce it. For example, in In re Madison 92nd St. Associates LLC, a prepayment premium was upheld under New York law because the agreement contained clear language requiring payment even after default and acceleration. Paloian v. LaSalle Bank N.A (In re Doctors Hosp. of Hyde Park, Inc.), 508 B.R. 697 (2014).
opinion_block: Prepayment premiums are generally enforceable under the New York common law 'rule of perfect tender in time.' [...] Where . . . a clear and unambiguous clause requires the payment of the prepayment premium even after default and acceleration, the clause will be analyzed as a liquidated damages clause. In re Madison 92nd St. Associates LLC, 472 B.R. 189, 195-96 (Bankr. S.D.N.Y. 2012).
finding_analysis: Paloian does quote the exact Madison 92nd Street passage the brief paraphrases, so using Paloian as a pincite for the Madison 92nd Street proposition is defensible as an 'example' citation. However, Paloian itself does not uphold a prepayment premium under Madison 92nd Street's 'clear and unambiguous' exception -- Judge Schmetterer instead holds the Yield Maintenance Premium is unmatured interest under Section 502(b)(2) and denies summary judgment only because of a factual dispute about whether the creditor was oversecured. Paloian also does not involve post-acceleration enforcement in Madison's sense; acceleration occurred post-petition when LaSalle filed foreclosure. So Paloian supports the background rule that clear language allowing post-acceleration premiums is enforceable under New York law, but readers could be misled to think Paloian itself enforced such a premium.

proposition: If the debtor is solvent, courts may apply the solvent-debtor exception to honor contractual obligations, including make-whole premiums, despite the general disallowance of unmatured interest under Section 502(b)(2).
cited_case: Ultra Petroleum Corp. v. Ad Hoc Comm., 943 F.3d 758 (2019)
retrieved_case: Ultra Petroleum Corporation v. Ad Hoc Commi
brief_sentence: Solvent Debtor Exception: If the debtor is solvent, courts may apply the "solvent-debtor exception" to honor contractual obligations, including make-whole premiums, despite the general disallowance of unmatured interest under ( 11 USCS § 502. b)(2) . 11-1-VI: S.D.N.Y. Bankruptcy Court Continues Trend Against Enforcing Make-Whole Premiums, Ultra Petroleum Corp. v. Ad Hoc Comm., 943 F.3d 758 (2019).
brief_block: Solvent Debtor Exception: If the debtor is solvent, courts may apply the 'solvent-debtor exception' to honor contractual obligations, including make-whole premiums, despite the general disallowance of unmatured interest under (11 USCS Section 502.b)(2). [...] Ultra Petroleum Corp. v. Ad Hoc Comm., 943 F.3d 758 (2019).
opinion_block: Our review of the record reveals no reason why the solvent-debtor exception could not apply. As other circuits have recognized, 'absent compelling equitable considerations, when a debtor is solvent, it is the role of the bankruptcy court to enforce the creditors' contractual rights.' [...] But 'mindful that we are a court of review, not of first view,' we will not make the choice ourselves or weigh the equities on our own. [...] Accordingly, the bankruptcy court should consider the Make-Whole Amount, the appropriate post-petition interest rate, and the applicability of the solvent-debtor exception on remand.
finding_analysis: The 2019 Ultra Petroleum opinion does raise the solvent-debtor exception and expressly leaves the door open to its application, stating it sees 'no reason why the solvent-debtor exception could not apply' and citing Dow Corning and Chicago, Milwaukee for the principle that courts should enforce creditors' contractual rights when the debtor is solvent. That broadly supports the brief's hedged 'courts may apply' framing. However, the Fifth Circuit did not hold that the exception applies; it remanded, explicitly declining to 'make the choice ourselves or weigh the equities,' and cautioned that any equitable power must be exercised 'within the confines of the Bankruptcy Code' (quoting Law v. Siegel). The brief's proposition is directionally accurate but overstates Ultra Petroleum as authority for applying the exception when it is really authority only for preserving the question on remand.

proposition: If the make-whole premium is deemed the economic equivalent of unmatured interest, it is likely to be disallowed under Section 502(b)(2), regardless of its characterization as liquidated damages or a separate contractual right.
cited_case: Wells Fargo Bank, N.A. v. Hertz Corp. (In re Hertz Corp.), 637 B.R. 781 (2021)
retrieved_case: Wells Fargo Bank, National Association v. The Hertz Corporation

brief_sentence: Courts also examine the economic substance of the make-whole premium to determine whether it constitutes unmatured interest. If the premium is deemed the economic equivalent of unmatured interest, it is likely to be disallowed under ( 11 USCS § 502. b)(2), regardless of its characterization as liquidated damages or a separate contractual right . Wells Fargo Bank, N.A. v. Hertz Corp. (In re Hertz Corp.), 637 B.R. 781 (2021), 6.04 Delaware Court in Energy Future Holdings Case Follows SDNY in Denying Make-Whole Premium after Acceleration Based on Plain Language of Loan Documents.
brief_block: Courts also examine the economic substance of the make-whole premium to determine whether it constitutes unmatured interest. If the premium is deemed the economic equivalent of unmatured interest, it is likely to be disallowed under 11 U.S.C. Section 502(b)(2), regardless of its characterization as liquidated damages or a separate contractual right. Wells Fargo Bank, N.A. v. Hertz Corp. (In re Hertz Corp.), 637 B.R. 781 (2021) [...]
opinion_block: Calling a make-whole a contract right or a liquidated damages provision does not answer the question of whether it is unmatured interest. In deciding whether a charge is unmatured interest 'courts look to the economic substance of the transaction to determine what counts as interest.' Doctors Hosp., 508 B.R. at 705. If it were enough to just label a make-whole claim liquidated damages, damages for breach of contract, or a 'separate contract right' from the obligation to pay interest, then a contract providing that on default or redemption 'all unmatured interest' would be immediately due and payable could avoid the effect of section 502(b)(2) completely. This is contrary to the express provisions of the Code and, consequently, the Court concludes that the characterization of a make-whole as a contract right or liquidated damages is not dispositive.
finding_analysis: Judge Walrath's opinion squarely supports the cited proposition. She expressly adopted the 'economic substance' framework, holding that courts look to economic substance to determine what counts as interest and that calling a make-whole a 'contract right,' 'liquidated damages,' or 'separate contract right' does not immunize it from Section 502(b)(2) disallowance. The brief's formulation -- that characterization as liquidated damages or a separate contractual right does not save a make-whole if it is the economic equivalent of unmatured interest -- tracks Judge Walrath's reasoning almost verbatim. Strong attribution.
